articles.headline: One Stressful Event Can Break Your Brain's Ability to Connect the Dots
articles.body: You might ace a test after a high-pressure meeting, but struggle to actually use what you know. Now there's a brain scan study that explains why. Researchers at the University of Hamburg had 121 participants learn information before and after a stress event, then tested whether they could make inferences linking what they'd learned. Stressed participants remembered the individual facts just fine. But their hippocampus, the brain region that links memories together, failed to connect them. The stressed group treated related pieces of information as completely separate files. The result: they could recall, but couldn't reason.
articles.takeaway: After a stressful event, give yourself time before tackling work that requires connecting ideas or making judgment calls. Your memory isn't broken, but your brain's linking system is temporarily offline.
articles.pexelsQuery: stressed person thinking head in hands

articles.topic: marijuana sleep quality survey mixed results
articles.headline: One in Three Adults Say Cannabis Helps Them Sleep. Their Doctors Aren't So Sure.
articles.body: A new national survey from the American Academy of Sleep Medicine asked over 2,000 American adults about marijuana and sleep. A third said their sleep was better when they used it. But sleep medicine doctors are urging caution. Research on cannabis and sleep is deeply conflicted. One recent systematic review found cannabinoids improved self-reported sleep quality, but a separate study found that daily long-term cannabis users had more objective nighttime wakefulness. There are also documented concerns around daytime sleepiness, physical dependence, and withdrawal-related sleep disruption. The perception of better sleep may not match what's actually happening in the brain.
articles.takeaway: If you're relying on cannabis to fall asleep, ask yourself whether you're solving the problem or masking it. Poor sleep has a cause. Finding it matters more than numbing it.
articles.pexelsQuery: person lying awake in bed night

product.name: LectroFan White Noise Machine
product.blurb: Blocks ambient noise so your sleep is actually restorative.

articles.topic: wearables CGM chronic disease management real-world data
articles.headline: Your Wearable Is Becoming a Real Medical Tool. Here's What the Latest Data Shows.
articles.body: Wearable devices have quietly crossed a line from fitness gadgets into clinical-grade health tools. Medscape reports that findings from May 2026 showed combining digital education with continuous glucose monitoring produced meaningful improvements in chronic disease outcomes. Doctors are increasingly leaning on wearable data for monitoring and research because it offers something office visits can't: continuous real-world signal. When patients self-report blood pressure readings on paper, conditions vary wildly. A wearable captures what's actually happening every hour of every day. The challenge in 2026 isn't data collection. It's making the torrent of information actionable.
articles.takeaway: If you have a smartwatch or fitness tracker, share the data with your doctor. It adds context to a 10-minute appointment that a clipboard form never could.

product.name: Garmin Forerunner 255
product.blurb: The watch Chris trains with daily. Tracks every discipline without breaking the bank.

articles.topic: massage therapy muscle recovery athletic performance
articles.headline: Massage Still Wins. A Fresh Look at the Evidence Just Confirmed It.
articles.body: The American Massage Therapy Association spotlighted a wave of recent research making a clear case for hands-on recovery work. A 2025 randomized controlled trial of 150 athletes found that bi-weekly deep tissue massage over eight weeks improved performance, flexibility, and recovery. A separate 2025 study compared six common recovery methods including cold-water immersion, vibration therapy, and static stretching, and found massage therapy stood out for reducing key markers of muscle damage and inflammation. A further RCT found that percussion massage therapy outperformed static stretching for soreness relief, with longer sessions delivering greater benefit.
articles.takeaway: If soreness is slowing you down, structured massage or even a quality percussion tool used consistently beats stretching alone. Twenty minutes matters more than you think.

product.name: Theragun Mini Massage Gun
product.blurb: Pocket-sized recovery. Takes the edge off sore muscles anywhere.

quote.text: The mind is the athlete. The body is simply the means it uses.
quote.author: Bryce Courtenay
quote.context: Australian author and advertising executive known for exploring human resilience and mental fortitude.

workout.title: 90-Second Charge: Short Burst Speed Builder
workout.tip: If you are running with a stroller or short on time, cut the intervals to 6 and keep the effort honest. Slowing down slightly is fine. Stopping the effort level is not.
workout.warmup: Start with 5 minutes of easy jogging at a very comfortable pace. Add 2 x 20-second strides at the end of the warmup, running smooth and relaxed at about 80 percent effort to get your legs turning over before the intervals begin.

exercises.name: Easy Jog Warmup
exercises.notes: Conversational pace. You should be able to say a full sentence without gasping.

exercises.name: 90-Second Hard Interval
exercises.reps: 90 sec on, 90 sec easy jog recovery
exercises.notes: Hard means uncomfortable but controlled. A 7 out of 10 effort. Not a sprint. You should feel like you could do one more rep after the last one.

exercises.name: Easy Jog Cooldown
exercises.notes: Drop back to warmup pace. Let your breathing settle fully before you stop moving.

workout.cooldown: Walk for 2-3 minutes after the cooldown jog. Shake out your legs and take slow deep breaths. Your heart rate should be below 100 bpm before you sit down.

product.name: HOKA Clifton 9
product.blurb: Max cushion for easy days and long runs. Keeps your legs fresh for hard sessions.

meal.title: Seared Tuna and Farro Bowl with Avocado and Lemon Herb Dressing
ingredients: Salt, black pepper, and chili flakes to taste
meal.instructions: 1. Season tuna steaks with salt, pepper, and chili flakes. Heat 1 tbsp olive oil in a pan over high heat and sear tuna for 90 seconds per side. Remove and slice. 2. Whisk remaining olive oil, lemon juice, Dijon mustard, parsley, salt, and pepper into a dressing. 3. Build bowls with farro as the base, then spinach, cherry tomatoes, and avocado. 4. Lay sliced tuna on top and drizzle with lemon herb dressing.
meal.tip: Cook farro in bulk on Sunday and refrigerate. This bowl comes together in under 10 minutes when the grain is already done.

product.name: Greater Goods Digital Food Scale
product.blurb: Measure your food for two weeks and you will understand your body better than any diet book.
